flashcards.Back: The primary rule a router uses to select a route from the routing table. it chooses the entry with the most specific (longest) subnet mask that matches the destination IP.
A rating of the trustworthiness of a routing information source. it is the first criteria used when multiple sources provide a route to the same destination.
A value used by a specific routing protocol to determine the best path among multiple routes from the same source to the same destination.
The logic that if a router learns two identical prefixes with the same AD, it then compares the metrics to decide which one to install in the routing table.
A router's ability to install and use multiple paths to the same destination if they have the same AD and the same metric, allowing for load balancing.
The 'route of last resort' with a prefix length of zero. it is the least specific match and is only used if no other route in the table matches the destination.
The most specific possible routing entry, matching a single IP address. it always wins the longest prefix match over any subnet or summary route.
The slowest packet forwarding method where the router's main CPU must handle every packet individually, including a full routing table lookup.
A legacy caching method where the first packet of a flow is handled by the CPU, and subsequent packets are forwarded using a hardware cache entry.
The modern, high-speed packet forwarding mechanism used by Cisco routers. it pre-calculates forwarding info and stores it in specialized hardware tables.
The primary table used by CEF. it is a mirrored copy of the routing table optimized for extremely fast hardware-based lookups.
A CEF table that stores pre-computed Layer 2 header information for all directly connected neighbors, allowing the router to encapsulate packets instantly.
The internal hardware architecture of a router or switch that moves data from the ingress (input) interface to the egress (output) interface.
The process that occurs as a packet enters a router, where its destination IP is extracted and compared against the FIB to find the exit path.
The final stage of packet forwarding where the router adds the correct Layer 2 header (MAC address) and sends the packet out the exit interface.
A mandatory step in router forwarding where the Time-to-Live field in the IP header is reduced by 1. if it reaches 0, the packet is dropped.
The process where a router recomputes the IP header checksum after modifying the TTL field to ensure data integrity during forwarding.
The mathematical data structure often used by software-based routing lookups to efficiently find the longest prefix match among thousands of entries.
A forwarding decision step where the router verifies if the packet size is larger than the exit interface's maximum transmission unit.
The process where a router breaks a large packet into smaller pieces if it exceeds the MTU of the outgoing link (only supported in IPv4).
The error message sent back to the source host by a router if it drops a packet because the TTL field reached zero during the forwarding process.
The error message sent by a router if it has no match (including no default route) in its routing table for a packet's destination IP.
The formal name for the master routing table that contains all paths learned from all sources. it is used to populate the FIB.
The overall set of rules a device follows: 1. Check L2 destination. 2. Check L3 destination (LPM). 3. Adjust TTL/Checksum. 4. Encapsulate L2. 5. Transmit.
The number of times a router must re-search the routing table to resolve a next-hop IP to a physical interface. High depth slows down forwarding.
The 'intelligence' of the router where protocols like OSPF and EIGRP run to build the routing table (RIB).
The hardware-driven part of the router (CEF/FIB) that performs the actual high-speed switching of packets from port to port.
A CEF state where the router knows a neighbor is directly connected but doesn't have its MAC address yet, triggering an ARP request.
The maximum number of Layer 2 neighbor entries that can be stored in hardware memory, which can limit the number of connected hosts.
The time delay between a packet entering an ingress port and exiting the egress port during the forwarding process.
